nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving or still, shallow, heavily vegetated surface waters; flooded forest margins, blackwater streams, and the edges of larger rivers

diet.notes: An insectivore in the wild — feeds on fallen insects, larvae, and zooplankton at the water surface. In captivity tends to accept small floating flakes, freeze-dried bloodworms, frozen brine shrimp, blackworms, and mosquito larvae. Surface-feeding approach is important as this species tends to ignore food that has sunk away from the top zone. Live or frozen insect-based foods are appreciated and help maintain condition.

goodWith: small peaceful tetras (cardinal tetra, neon tetra, ember tetra)
avoidWith: aggressive or fast-swimming tankmates that outcompete at the surface

summary: A small, distinctive freshwater surface dweller with a deep-keeled, hatchet-shaped body. Peaceful schooling fish; an accomplished jumper requiring the tightest possible lid — even a small gap is an escape opportunity.
careNotes: The Common Hatchetfish is a surface-exclusive species with a uniquely deep, keel-shaped body adapted for gliding and jumping. It spends virtually all its time in the top zone of the water column and tends not to compete with mid- or bottom-dwelling fish for space or food, making it a compatible addition to community tanks with appropriate tankmates. The single most critical husbandry requirement is a tightly-sealed aquarium lid — this species has been reported to jump several body-lengths and will exploit the smallest gap; escape risk is high. A long tank footprint (rather than a tall or cube-format aquarium) is generally advised to provide adequate surface swimming space for the school. A minimum group of 6 is commonly recommended to encourage natural schooling behavior and reduce individual stress; larger groups of 10–12+ tend to display more confidently. Wild diet is predominantly fallen insects at the surface — supplement captive diet with surface-fed floating foods and frozen/freeze-dried insect-based offerings such as mosquito larvae, daphnia, or brine shrimp. Food that sinks is typically ignored. This species has been reported to be somewhat sensitive to deteriorating water quality and new-tank syndrome; a cycled, well-maintained aquarium and a careful quarantine period are generally advised before introduction. Soft, slightly acidic water closer to its Amazon blackwater origin is preferred, though the species has been documented tolerating a fairly wide range of conditions during seasonal floods in the wild. Breeding in home aquaria has not been reliably documented.
breedingNotes: Captive breeding has not been reliably documented. FishBase notes no successful captive breeding records. Spawning in the wild is associated with the rainy season and flooded vegetation. Not a commercially bred species; aquarium specimens are typically wild-caught.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked: adult size (max 4.0 cm SL / 6.5 cm TL), temperature range (23–27°C), pH (6.0–7.0), hardness (up to 15 dH), order (Characiformes), native range (Peruvian Amazon, middle Amazon, the Guianas, Venezuela, Paraguay basin), diet (worms, crustaceans, insects, aerial insect capture). SeriouslyFish (first secondary) returned HTTP 520; Practical Fishkeeping returned 403; FishBase used as replacement secondary. pH floor widened to 5.5 per user brief (softwater/blackwater habitats) and user-supplied expert data; gH floor lowered to 2 and kH 2–10 from user brief for same reason.

fish.breedingNotes: No reliable captive breeding protocol documented. FishBase records no successful captive breeding. Wild spawning tied to rainy-season flooding conditions not easily replicated in home aquaria.
